q: What is a robo-advisor and how do they create a personalized portfolio for a customer? What does it cost to use a robo-advisor?

options.A: A human financial planner who charges 2%-3% of AUM for bespoke stock picks.
options.B: An active fund manager who beats the market through security selection and timing.
options.C: An automated online portal whose algorithm maps risk answers to a low-fee ETF portfolio.
options.D: A brokerage app offering commission-free trading funded by payment for order flow.

q: What is passive investing and how does it explain the growth of exchange traded funds (ETFs)?

options.A: A timing strategy using technical analysis to beat indices, driving demand for leveraged ETFs.
options.B: Tracking broad market indices and minimizing fees, which fueled cheap index-tracking ETFs.
options.C: Selecting undervalued securities via mean-variance analysis, which mutual funds then packaged as ETFs.
options.D: Rebalancing portfolios daily for tax-loss harvesting, a service only ETFs can provide.

q: What is the business strategy of robo-advisors and what customers are they targeting?

options.A: A disruptive-innovation strategy targeting underserved millennials and price-sensitive mass-affluent investors.
options.B: A premium strategy targeting ultra-high-net-worth families needing dedicated family offices.
options.C: A B2B-first strategy licensing software to incumbent banks from the outset.
options.D: A high-frequency trading strategy targeting institutional investors seeking lower spreads.

q: What has been the experience of incumbent asset managers as they respond to the threat of disruption from robo-advisors?

options.A: They ignored the threat and ceded most robo assets to independent start-ups.
options.B: They were slow first-movers whose legacy systems prevented any robo offering.
options.C: They partnered exclusively with Wealthfront to white-label a shared platform.
options.D: They were fast-followers who built or licensed robos and captured most assets.

q: What are the main lessons from the David-vs.-Goliath battle between robo-advisors and incumbents?

options.A: Superior technology is a durable moat, so start-ups win once they out-code incumbents.
options.B: Acquiring customers is the biggest obstacle, technology isn't durable, and partnering can beat attacking.
options.C: Low fees alone guarantee scale, so cutting advisory fees to zero ensures survival.
options.D: First-mover advantage is decisive, so a seven-year head start ensured start-up dominance.

q: What is the wealth management industry, how big is it, and who works in it?

options.A: A $1.8 trillion industry consisting almost entirely of robo-advisors and their engineers.
options.B: A $10 trillion ETF-issuing industry run mainly by BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street.
options.C: A $100+ trillion industry spanning planning, investing, and operations with diverse professionals.
options.D: A $35 trillion sustainable-investing industry staffed largely by ESG research analysts.

q: What is the difference between institutional investors and retail customers? What are high net worth customers?

options.A: Institutional investors manage portfolios wholesale; retail are individuals; HNW hold $1 million or more.
options.B: Institutional investors are individual DIY savers; retail are pension funds; HNW hold $30 million or more.
options.C: Institutional investors are only paid on AUA; retail only on AUM; HNW means any registered advisor.
options.D: Institutional investors are family offices; retail are beneficial owners; HNW hold $100,000 or more.

q: What is meant by digital wealth management? What technologies are being used to transform this industry and how?

options.A: Replacing all advisors with robots that eliminate human involvement in wealth management.
options.B: A regtech-only approach that automates compliance while leaving investing entirely manual.
options.C: Using blockchain solely to tokenize ETFs and cut the third-party management expense ratio.
options.D: Leveraging cloud, big data, APIs, AI, and blockchain to automate tasks and improve experience.

q: What are examples of fintechs attacking different parts of the wealth management value chain?

options.A: Vanguard onboarding, Schwab custody, Fidelity reporting, and TD Ameritrade research.
options.B: Nest Wealth digital onboarding, Aladdin Wealth portfolio management, Clim8 ESG, Robinhood zero-commission trading.
options.C: Betterment tax-loss harvesting, Wealthfront family offices, SigFig custody, Acorns proxy voting.
options.D: Markowitz mean-variance tools, Bogle index funds, SPDR ETFs, and Bridgewater risk parity.

q: What is a technology-enabled eAdvisor, and why is a hybrid model so important for the future of the wealth management industry?

options.A: A fully automated robo requiring no humans; hybrid matters because it eliminates advisory fees.
options.B: An offshore back-office team; hybrid matters because it outsources compliance and KYC cheaply.
options.C: A tech-enabled advisor managing a larger book; hybrid matters because humans are supported, not replaced.
options.D: An AI chatbot replacing planners; hybrid matters because it removes behavioral biases entirely.
